name: css-development-refactor
description: This skill should be used when refactoring existing CSS from inline styles or utility classes to semantic patterns. Triggers on "refactor CSS", "extract styles", "consolidate CSS", "convert inline", "clean up styles", "migrate to semantic". Transforms to semantic classes with dark mode and tests.
capabilities: Extract inline styles to semantic CSS classes with automatic naming conventions
Consolidate repeated utility class combinations into reusable components using Tailwind @apply
Add dark mode variants to all refactored CSS classes automatically
Generate test coverage for CSS components including static CSS tests and rendering tests
Update markup across multiple files to use new semantic class names
Create documentation comments for each extracted CSS component with usage examples
author: 2389-research
securitySummary: This skill is a pure documentation resource for CSS refactoring workflows. All 71 static findings are false positives from markdown syntax being misinterpreted as executable code. The skill contains no actual scripts, network calls, or file operations, only examples in documentation blocks.
valueStatement: This skill transforms messy CSS from inline styles and repeated utility classes into organized semantic components. It extracts patterns, adds dark mode support, creates tests, and ensures maintainable styling with Tailwind composition.
userTitle: Refactor CSS to Semantic Component Patterns
limitations: Requires existing CSS to refactor and cannot create designs from scratch
Works best with Tailwind CSS projects and may need adaptation for other frameworks
Cannot automatically test visual appearance and requires manual verification of styling
Does not handle complex CSS animations or transitions beyond basic hover states

useCases.targetUser: Frontend developers maintaining large component libraries
useCases.title: Clean Up Utility Class Sprawl
useCases.description: Convert repeated utility class combinations in markup into semantic component classes to reduce code duplication and improve maintainability.

useCases.targetUser: Product teams adding dark mode support to existing applications
useCases.title: Add Dark Mode to Existing CSS
useCases.description: Refactor existing CSS components to include dark mode variants without breaking current functionality or requiring major rewrites.

useCases.targetUser: Teams modernizing legacy codebases or improving code quality
useCases.title: Migrate from Inline Styles to CSS Classes
useCases.description: Transform inline style attributes in React or HTML components into organized semantic CSS classes with proper naming and structure.

promptTemplates.title: Basic CSS Refactoring
promptTemplates.prompt: Refactor the button styles in this component to use semantic CSS classes
promptTemplates.scenario: When you have a component with repeated utility classes or inline styles that need organization

promptTemplates.title: Extract Repeated Patterns
promptTemplates.prompt: Find all repeated utility class combinations across these files and extract them to semantic components
promptTemplates.scenario: When you need to identify and consolidate duplicated styling patterns across multiple files

promptTemplates.title: Add Dark Mode Support
promptTemplates.prompt: Refactor these CSS classes to include dark mode variants following our design system
promptTemplates.scenario: When adding dark mode support to existing components without breaking current functionality

promptTemplates.title: Complete Refactoring with Tests
promptTemplates.prompt: Refactor all card components to semantic CSS, add dark mode support, create tests, and verify behavior is unchanged
promptTemplates.scenario: When you need a comprehensive refactoring with full testing and verification workflow

bestPractices: Always analyze existing components before creating new classes to avoid duplication and ensure consistency with established patterns
Include dark mode variants for all refactored classes to maintain feature parity and prepare for theme switching
Verify behavior is unchanged after refactoring by running tests and visually inspecting components in both light and dark modes
antiPatterns: Creating new semantic classes without checking if similar patterns already exist in the codebase, leading to duplicate components
Refactoring CSS without adding test coverage, making it difficult to verify behavior is preserved and catch regressions
Skipping dark mode variants during refactoring and planning to add them later, creating technical debt and inconsistent theming

faq.question: Does this skill work with CSS frameworks other than Tailwind?
faq.answer: The skill is optimized for Tailwind CSS and uses Tailwind @apply directives. It can adapt to other frameworks but may require manual adjustments to the refactoring workflow and syntax.

faq.question: Will refactoring change how my components look?
faq.answer: No. The skill performs behavior-neutral refactoring, meaning visual output should match exactly. It includes verification steps to ensure styling is preserved during the transformation.

faq.question: How does the skill decide which patterns to extract?
faq.answer: It identifies repeated utility class combinations appearing 2 or more times, checks if similar semantic classes already exist, and prioritizes high-frequency patterns for extraction to maximize code reduction.

faq.question: Can I use this skill on individual components or does it require full project access?
faq.answer: You can use it on individual components or specific files. The skill will analyze only the provided scope and suggest extractions based on patterns found within that scope.

faq.question: What testing does the skill add after refactoring?
faq.answer: It adds static CSS tests to verify classes exist and contain expected properties, plus component rendering tests to ensure classes are applied correctly. You should still manually verify visual appearance.

faq.question: How does dark mode support work if my project does not have dark mode yet?
faq.answer: The skill adds dark: variants following Tailwind conventions, preparing components for dark mode even if not currently enabled. These variants remain dormant until dark mode is activated in your project.
